title: Internet Sales: Virtual Corporations, Real Profit
author: Chris Stevens (Aberdeen Group)
abstract: Aberdeen Group's 170-page report by Chris Stevens advises managers and executives on opening an Internet sales channel, covering market potential assessment, commerce application platform selection, and emerging online marketing strategies. The study reviews the state of Internet business in 1997, covers mainstream consumer adoption patterns, digital payment systems, and Commerce Application Platforms (CAPs), and identifies key technology trends including bandwidth constraints and security imperatives. It specifically excludes EDI and private financial networks, positioning itself as a companion to Aberdeen's supply-side electronic commerce report.
importance_rationale: One of the first comprehensive frameworks for corporate Internet channel strategy published by a major analyst firm; the Commerce Application Platform taxonomy and the virtual corporation model shaped how early e-commerce platforms were evaluated and selected.
relevance_rationale: The strategic framework for evaluating Internet channel viability remains applicable; the CAP vendor landscape has consolidated dramatically (most named vendors no longer exist independently) but the evaluation criteria — online market potential, platform choice, marketing strategy — are still used.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's bullish prediction on Internet sales growth proved correct in aggregate, though many specific vendor-level viability assessments proved wrong as the dot-com crash (2000-2001) eliminated or consolidated most early CAP vendors; the underlying demand thesis was highly accurate.
